learning_objectives: Apply strategic planning frameworks (SWOT, PESTEL, Porter's Five Forces) to communications contexts
Develop a situation analysis that grounds communications strategy in organizational reality
Distinguish between strategic plans, tactical plans, and campaign plans
readings: Smith, Strategic Planning for Public Relations, Ch. 1-3
Mintzberg, 'The Fall and Rise of Strategic Planning' (Harvard Business Review)

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategic communication planning is the capstone discipline that integrates everything you have learned. It connects organizational objectives to communication activities through a structured planning process that ensures every message, channel, and campaign serves a larger purpose.
key_concepts: Strategic planning hierarchy: organizational strategy, communications strategy, tactical plans, campaign plans
Situation analysis: SWOT (internal strengths/weaknesses, external opportunities/threats), PESTEL (political, economic, social, technological, environmental, legal factors)
Stakeholder analysis: mapping audiences by influence, interest, and information needs
Goal cascade: organizational objectives flowing down to communications objectives flowing down to tactical KPIs
Environmental scanning: systematic monitoring of trends, competitors, and market shifts that inform strategy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a complete situation analysis for a university's communications department: SWOT analysis, stakeholder mapping, competitive audit, and environmental scan, synthesized into strategic priorities

review.value: A hierarchical alignment system where organizational goals (increase enrollment 10%) inform communications goals (increase brand awareness among high school juniors) which inform tactical KPIs (generate 50,000 website visits from target demographic)
A framework analyzing macro-environmental factors: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, and Legal forces that shape the context in which communications strategies must operate

learning_objectives: Write SMART communications objectives that connect to business outcomes
Design KPI frameworks with leading and lagging indicators
Build measurement plans that prove communications ROI to organizational leadership
readings: Smith, Strategic Planning for Public Relations, Ch. 5-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Communications objectives must be specific enough to guide action and measurable enough to prove value. Vague goals like 'increase brand awareness' or 'improve social media presence' are not objectives. They are wishes. This session transforms wishes into measurable objectives.
key_concepts: SMART objectives: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound applied to communications
Leading vs. lagging indicators: inputs and activities (leading) vs. outcomes and results (lagging)
The measurement pyramid: activity metrics at the base, outcome metrics in the middle, business impact at the top
ROI calculation for communications: attributing revenue and cost savings to communications activities
Reporting for leadership: translating communications metrics into language executives understand and value
lecture_notes.demonstration: Transforming five vague communications goals into SMART objectives with defined KPIs, measurement methods, and reporting formats, then creating a one-page measurement dashboard for executive presentation

review.value: Leading indicators (content published, pitches sent, ads launched) predict future outcomes and are controllable. Lagging indicators (media coverage, website traffic, conversions) measure results after the fact. Both are needed: leading indicators for real-time optimization, lagging indicators for performance evaluation.

Return on investment calculated as (value of outcomes attributable to communications - cost of communications activities) / cost of communications activities; requires clear attribution methodology and agreed-upon value definitions

learning_objectives: Segment audiences using demographic, psychographic, behavioral, and needs-based criteria
Develop research-informed audience personas that guide content and channel decisions
Map audience journeys from awareness through advocacy with communications touchpoints
readings: Smith, Strategic Planning for Public Relations, Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategy without audience understanding is guessing. The more precisely you define and understand your audiences, the more effectively you can allocate limited communications resources. Generic messages to generic audiences produce generic results.
key_concepts: Segmentation criteria: demographics, psychographics, behaviors, needs, and media consumption patterns
Persona development: creating semi-fictional audience representations based on research, not assumptions
Audience journey mapping: tracing the path from unaware to aware to interested to committed to advocate
Channel-audience alignment: matching communication channels to where each audience actually consumes information
Persona-driven content planning: using personas to guide content topics, formats, tones, and distribution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Developing three audience personas for a nonprofit fundraising campaign: conducting secondary research, defining demographic and psychographic profiles, mapping each persona's information journey, and identifying optimal channels and messages

review.value: Personas grounded in actual data (surveys, interviews, analytics) rather than assumptions; a persona built on what you think the audience cares about is an exercise in imagination, while a persona built on research is a strategic tool

Tracing each audience segment's path from first awareness through engagement, conversion, and advocacy, identifying the communications touchpoints, information needs, and potential barriers at each stage

learning_objectives: Evaluate and select communication channels based on audience reach, cost, and strategic fit
Allocate limited budgets and staff time across channels for maximum impact
Design integrated channel strategies where channels complement rather than duplicate each other
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing, Ch. 4 - Channel Strategy

lecture_notes.introduction: You cannot do everything everywhere. Strategic channel selection, acknowledging that some channels matter more than others for your specific objectives and audiences, is one of the most important decisions in a communications plan. Saying no to a channel is often more strategic than saying yes.
key_concepts: Channel evaluation criteria: audience presence, reach potential, engagement quality, cost efficiency, content format fit
The 70-20-10 budget rule: 70% on proven channels, 20% on emerging opportunities, 10% on experiments
Channel integration: designing cross-channel experiences where each channel has a distinct role
Resource allocation: matching channel ambition to available staff time, budget, and expertise
Channel sunset decisions: knowing when to stop investing in a channel that no longer serves the strategy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Allocating a $150,000 annual communications budget across eight potential channels for a B2B technology company: evaluating each channel against audience data, selecting five, determining budget allocation, and justifying the two rejected channels

review.value: Allocating 70% of budget to proven, high-performing channels, 20% to promising channels with emerging evidence, and 10% to experimental channels for innovation; this balances reliable results with strategic growth
The deliberate decision to stop investing in a channel that no longer reaches the target audience effectively or provides adequate return, freeing resources for higher-performing or emerging channels

learning_objectives: Structure a strategic communications plan presentation for executive audiences
Anticipate and address leadership objections to proposed strategies
Translate communications strategy into language that connects to business outcomes
readings: Harvard Business Review, 'How to Present to the C-Suite'

lecture_notes.introduction: A brilliant strategy that cannot be communicated to leadership is indistinguishable from no strategy at all. The ability to present, defend, and sell your strategic plan to executives who care about business outcomes, not communications jargon, is the ultimate professional skill.
key_concepts: Executive presentation structure: situation, strategy, expected outcomes, resource requirements, risks, timeline
The business case: framing communications investments as business investments with projected returns
Objection handling: preparing for budget challenges, ROI skepticism, and 'why can't we just...' questions
Visual storytelling in presentations: data visualization, before/after scenarios, competitive positioning
The one-page strategic summary: condensing a 30-page plan into a single-page executive brief
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student delivers a 5-minute strategic plan pitch to a panel playing executive roles, followed by 5 minutes of questions where the panel challenges assumptions, budget, and projected outcomes

review.value: Presenting communications strategy using business language: investment (not cost), projected return (not impressions), risk mitigation (not reputation), and competitive advantage (not brand awareness); executives fund business cases, not communications plans

A single page capturing situation, objectives, audiences, strategy, channels, budget, timeline, and expected outcomes; forces clarity and enables executives to make decisions without reading the full plan

questions.question: Explain the concept of a goal cascade and provide an example showing how an organizational goal becomes a communications objective and then a tactical KPI.
questions.answer: A goal cascade aligns communications work to organizational priorities through hierarchical objectives. Example: Organizational goal: Increase annual revenue by 15%. Communications objective: Generate 10,000 qualified leads from digital channels by Q4 (specific, measurable contribution to revenue). Tactical KPI: Achieve 5% conversion rate on landing pages from paid social campaigns, generating 500 leads per month. Each level is more specific and actionable while remaining traceable to the level above it.

questions.question: Distinguish between leading and lagging indicators in communications measurement and provide one example of each.
questions.answer: Leading indicators measure activities and inputs that predict future outcomes and are directly controllable (e.g., number of blog posts published per week). Lagging indicators measure results and outcomes after they occur (e.g., organic website traffic growth). Leading indicators are useful for real-time course correction because you can change them immediately. Lagging indicators are useful for evaluating whether the strategy worked. A complete measurement plan needs both: leading indicators to manage execution and lagging indicators to evaluate effectiveness.

questions.question: What is the difference between a research-based persona and an assumption-based persona, and why does the distinction matter for strategic planning?
questions.answer: A research-based persona is built from actual data: surveys, interviews, analytics, customer research, and behavioral data that reveal what the audience actually thinks, needs, and does. An assumption-based persona is built from what the team believes about the audience based on experience or intuition. The distinction matters because strategies built on assumptions optimize for what you think works rather than what actually works. Assumption-based personas often reflect the team's own demographics and preferences rather than the target audience's reality, leading to misallocated channels, wrong tone, and irrelevant content.

rubric.key: Explains why the distinction matters strategically
Notes the risk of assumption-based planning

questions.question: Describe the 70-20-10 budget framework and explain when you would deviate from it.
questions.answer: The 70-20-10 framework allocates 70% of budget to proven high-performing channels, 20% to promising emerging channels, and 10% to experimental channels. Deviation is appropriate when: (1) A startup or new brand has no proven channels yet, requiring a higher experimental allocation (perhaps 40-40-20) until data identifies winners. (2) A mature brand in a disrupted market needs to invest more heavily in emerging channels because proven channels are declining (perhaps 50-30-20). (3) During a crisis or major strategic pivot when existing channels need to be replaced quickly. The framework is a starting point for disciplined allocation, not a rigid rule.

rubric.key: Provides a valid scenario for deviation
Demonstrates strategic thinking about when rules should flex

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Strategic Communications Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive strategic communications plan for a real organization, including situation analysis, objectives, audience strategy, channel plan, content strategy, budget allocation, timeline, and measurement framework. Present the plan in a professional executive pitch.
objectives: Conduct a thorough situation analysis using established strategic frameworks
Develop SMART objectives with cascading KPIs
Create research-informed audience personas and channel strategies
Present the plan persuasively to a simulated executive audience
deliverables: Strategic communications plan (15-20 pages) with all components
Executive summary (1 page) suitable for leadership review
Measurement framework with KPIs, reporting cadence, and success thresholds

criteria: SWOT and environmental analysis are thorough and evidence-based
Competitive landscape is accurately mapped
Key challenges and opportunities are clearly identified
Analysis directly informs strategic recommendations
Objectives are SMART and cascade properly from organizational goals
Audience personas are research-informed
Channel strategy is justified by audience data
Content strategy aligns with objectives and audience needs
Budget allocation is realistic and justified
Timeline is detailed and achievable
Resource requirements are honestly assessed
Risk mitigation plans are included
Presentation is structured for an executive audience
Business case is compelling and grounded in projected outcomes
Q&A responses demonstrate deep strategic understanding
One-page summary effectively captures the full plan
Rigorous situation analysis grounding strategy in organizational reality
SMART objectives with clear audience and channel strategies
Realistic execution plan with justified resource allocation
Persuasive executive presentation demonstrating strategic mastery

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on strategic planning concepts and frameworks

weekly_exercises.description: Weekly strategic analysis, planning, and presentation exercises

major_assignment.description: Comprehensive strategic communications plan with executive presentation

midterm_examination.description: Written examination on strategic planning frameworks and methodology

final_presentation.description: Final strategic plan defense before faculty panel

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all sessions. Strategic workshops and presentation sessions require participation. More than 2 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Presentation dates cannot be rescheduled without advance arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Strategic analysis may be discussed among peers. All plans and presentations are individual. Sharing strategy documents is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All research must cite sources. Organizational data used in plans must be accurate. Fabricating market research or competitive data is academic fraud.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need presentation software, spreadsheets for budget planning, and access to research databases. Strategic planning templates are provided on LMS.
